However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=draft|12=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Imperial decrees]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Senmyō}}

'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Imperial decrees]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Senmyō}}

revision.comment: 45 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宣命 up to rev 102721280
revision.text:
'''宣命'''（せんみょう）とは、[[天皇]]の命令を[[漢字]]だけの[[日本語|和文]]体で記した文書であり、[[漢文]]体の[[詔勅]]に対していう。この文体を'''[[#宣命体・宣命書|宣命体]]'''（-たい）、その表記法を'''[[#宣命体・宣命書|宣命書]]'''（-がき）、また宣命を読み上げる使者を'''宣命使'''（-し）、宣命を記す紙を'''[[#宣命紙|宣命紙]]'''（-し）という。宣命体は、[[仮名混じり文|漢字仮名交じり文]]の源泉となった文体で、かなの発達史上、大変重要な文書である。 == 概要 == 天皇が宣（の）りたまう大命（おおみこと、命令）の意で、本来は口頭で宣布され、それを宣命体で書記した。[[奈良時代]]は[[朝賀]]・[[即位]]・[[改元]]・[[立后]]・[[立太子]]などの儀式に用いられ、[[平安時代]]以降は[[任大臣儀|任大臣]]・[[贈位]]・[[神社]]・[[天皇陵|山陵]]などの告文にだけ用いられた。 === 宣命体・宣命書 === 宣命・[[祝詞]]現在でも[[祝詞]]では宣命体が用いられており、祭りや祈願の際に神官によって記されている。などの文体を宣命体といい、その表記法である宣命書とは、[[品詞|体言]]・[[品詞|用言]]の語幹を大きな字で書き、[[助詞]]・[[助動詞 (国文法)|助動詞]]・用言の[[語尾|活用語尾]]などは、一字一音の[[万葉仮名]]で小さく右に寄せて書く方法である。「を」には「乎」、「の」には「乃」、「は」には「波」などを一定して使っている。ただし、宣命体には2種類ある。助詞なども含めてすべて大字で書かれる'''宣命大書体'''（せんみょう だいしょたい）と、上述のように助詞などを小字で書き分ける'''宣命小書体'''（せんみょう しょうしょたい）である。 宣命書は、「漢字万葉仮名交じり文」と言えるが、その万葉仮名を[[平仮名]]に変えると、「漢字仮名交じり文」とほぼ同じになり、これは[[日本語]]表記の展開史の上で注目すべき出来事であった大島正二『漢字伝来』P.113 - 114。 === 宣命体のモデル === 古代[[朝鮮]]の[[吏読]]（りとう）が宣命体を導いたと推測されている。文型上、[[朝鮮語]]は日本語とよく似た[[言語]]で、助詞や助動詞などの文法的要素を必要とするため、漢文で書かれていた古代朝鮮の公文書にこれらの朝鮮語を書き加えることがおこってくる。これを吏読というが、原則として漢文の各分節の終わりにその要素を送りがなのように漢字で記した。この方法は日本の宣命書と同じであり、ここに吏読が宣命体のモデルという推測が生まれた大島正二『漢字伝来』P.117。ただ、仮名を小さく書く宣命小書体に進展したのは、日本における成果である森岡隆『図説かなの成り立ち事典』P.187。 === 宣命紙 === {{See also|麻紙}} 宣命を記す紙で、普通は黄麻紙（おうまし）を用い、[[伊勢神宮]]には[[縹]]色の紙、[[賀茂神社]]には紅色の紙を用いた新村出『広辞苑』。 黄麻紙とは、[[麻 (繊維)|麻]]を主体に造った紙で、[[害虫]]を防ぐために[[黄檗]]（おうばく）で染めたため、その色からこの名がある。古来より麻は清浄なものとされていたので、奈良時代の[[写経]]にも多く使用された。 {{Cite book|和書|author=二玄社編集部|title=書道辞典|publisher=二玄社;|date=2010|isbn=978-4544120080|page=30}} == 宣命 == === 孝謙天皇宣命 === 『[[続日本紀]]』には、697年、[[文武天皇]]の即位時のものをはじめとする宣命が62編、収録されている。しかし、いずれも原本は現存しない。 [[天平勝宝]]9歳（757年）3月25日の[[孝謙天皇]]宣命の転写が[[正倉院]]文書・正集第44巻に収めされている『[[続日本紀]]』には収録されていない。[[孝謙天皇]]宣命の転写はもう1点あり、[[正倉院]]文書・続修第一巻に収められている。。 {| class="wikitable" |+ 孝謙天皇宣命（部分） |- ! 原文 !! 釈文 |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | 天皇（すめら）が大命（おほみこと）らまと宣（のりたま）ふ大命を
衆（もろもろ）聞食（きこしめさ）へと宣（のる）。
此（こ）の天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天（あめ）の賜（たま）へる大（おほい）なる
瑞（しるし）を頂（いただき）に受賜（うけたま）はり |} === 後伏見上皇宣命 === [[後鳥羽天皇]]が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の院宣（宣命）で践祚して以降、上皇による院宣（宣命）によって新天皇が践祚することができるようになった。次のものは、[[後醍醐天皇]]が笠置山に立て籠もったことによって、[[後伏見上皇]]が皇太子量仁親王（[[光厳天皇]]）の践祚を命じた宣命である宮内庁書陵部編『図書寮叢刊 壬生家文書 六』（昭和59年），p.27。 {{Quotation|　'''後伏見上皇宣命案'''

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[宣命]] to [[Senmyō]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''宣命'''（せんみょう）とは、[[天皇]]の命令を[[漢字]]だけの[[日本語|和文]]体で記した文書であり、[[漢文]]体の[[詔勅]]に対していう。この文体を'''[[#宣命体・宣命書|宣命体]]'''（-たい）、その表記法を'''[[#宣命体・宣命書|宣命書]]'''（-がき）、また宣命を読み上げる使者を'''宣命使'''（-し）、宣命を記す紙を'''[[#宣命紙|宣命紙]]'''（-し）という。宣命体は、[[仮名混じり文|漢字仮名交じり文]]の源泉となった文体で、かなの発達史上、大変重要な文書である。 == 概要 == 天皇が宣（の）りたまう大命（おおみこと、命令）の意で、本来は口頭で宣布され、それを宣命体で書記した。[[奈良時代]]は[[朝賀]]・[[即位]]・[[改元]]・[[立后]]・[[立太子]]などの儀式に用いられ、[[平安時代]]以降は[[任大臣儀|任大臣]]・[[贈位]]・[[神社]]・[[天皇陵|山陵]]などの告文にだけ用いられた。 === 宣命体・宣命書 === 宣命・[[祝詞]]現在でも[[祝詞]]では宣命体が用いられており、祭りや祈願の際に神官によって記されている。などの文体を宣命体といい、その表記法である宣命書とは、[[品詞|体言]]・[[品詞|用言]]の語幹を大きな字で書き、[[助詞]]・[[助動詞 (国文法)|助動詞]]・用言の[[語尾|活用語尾]]などは、一字一音の[[万葉仮名]]で小さく右に寄せて書く方法である。「を」には「乎」、「の」には「乃」、「は」には「波」などを一定して使っている。ただし、宣命体には2種類ある。助詞なども含めてすべて大字で書かれる'''宣命大書体'''（せんみょう だいしょたい）と、上述のように助詞などを小字で書き分ける'''宣命小書体'''（せんみょう しょうしょたい）である。 宣命書は、「漢字万葉仮名交じり文」と言えるが、その万葉仮名を[[平仮名]]に変えると、「漢字仮名交じり文」とほぼ同じになり、これは[[日本語]]表記の展開史の上で注目すべき出来事であった大島正二『漢字伝来』P.113 - 114。 === 宣命体のモデル === 古代[[朝鮮]]の[[吏読]]（りとう）が宣命体を導いたと推測されている。文型上、[[朝鮮語]]は日本語とよく似た[[言語]]で、助詞や助動詞などの文法的要素を必要とするため、漢文で書かれていた古代朝鮮の公文書にこれらの朝鮮語を書き加えることがおこってくる。これを吏読というが、原則として漢文の各分節の終わりにその要素を送りがなのように漢字で記した。この方法は日本の宣命書と同じであり、ここに吏読が宣命体のモデルという推測が生まれた大島正二『漢字伝来』P.117。ただ、仮名を小さく書く宣命小書体に進展したのは、日本における成果である森岡隆『図説かなの成り立ち事典』P.187。 === 宣命紙 === {{See also|麻紙}} 宣命を記す紙で、普通は黄麻紙（おうまし）を用い、[[伊勢神宮]]には[[縹]]色の紙、[[賀茂神社]]には紅色の紙を用いた新村出『広辞苑』。 黄麻紙とは、[[麻 (繊維)|麻]]を主体に造った紙で、[[害虫]]を防ぐために[[黄檗]]（おうばく）で染めたため、その色からこの名がある。古来より麻は清浄なものとされていたので、奈良時代の[[写経]]にも多く使用された。 {{Cite book|和書|author=二玄社編集部|title=書道辞典|publisher=二玄社;|date=2010|isbn=978-4544120080|page=30}} == 宣命 == === 孝謙天皇宣命 === 『[[続日本紀]]』には、697年、[[文武天皇]]の即位時のものをはじめとする宣命が62編、収録されている。しかし、いずれも原本は現存しない。 [[天平勝宝]]9歳（757年）3月25日の[[孝謙天皇]]宣命の転写が[[正倉院]]文書・正集第44巻に収めされている『[[続日本紀]]』には収録されていない。[[孝謙天皇]]宣命の転写はもう1点あり、[[正倉院]]文書・続修第一巻に収められている。。 {| class="wikitable" |+ 孝謙天皇宣命（部分） |- ! 原文 !! 釈文 |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | 天皇（すめら）が大命（おほみこと）らまと宣（のりたま）ふ大命を
衆（もろもろ）聞食（きこしめさ）へと宣（のる）。
此（こ）の天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天（あめ）の賜（たま）へる大（おほい）なる
瑞（しるし）を頂（いただき）に受賜（うけたま）はり |} === 後伏見上皇宣命 === [[後鳥羽天皇]]が[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]の院宣（宣命）で践祚して以降、上皇による院宣（宣命）によって新天皇が践祚することができるようになった。次のものは、[[後醍醐天皇]]が笠置山に立て籠もったことによって、[[後伏見上皇]]が皇太子量仁親王（[[光厳天皇]]）の践祚を命じた宣命である宮内庁書陵部編『図書寮叢刊 壬生家文書 六』（昭和59年），p.27。 {{Quotation|　'''後伏見上皇宣命案'''

'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Imperial decrees]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Senmyō}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Senmyō}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Imperial decrees]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]]

Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man’yōgana|qq=}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Imperial decrees]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]]

revision.text:
'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man’yōgana|ja|万葉仮名|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man’yōgana|qq=}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Senmyō]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]]

However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]] [[Category:Diplomatics]]

'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese history]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[en:Senmyō]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]]

This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[sn:Senmyō]] [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]]
