This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}}

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|qq=|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|qq=|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}}

The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}}

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}}

瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Historical documents]] }} [[ja:宣命]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], in contrast to [[:en:Edict|edictal texts]] written in [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]]. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with [[:en:Hiragana|Hiragana]], it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient [[:en:Korean|Korean]] administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as [[:en:sky blue|sky blue]] for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Red|Red]] for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]] and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Go-Toba]] ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]], subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later [[:en:Emperor Kōgon|Emperor Kōgon]]). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man’yōgana]] * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with {{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|WD=Q48332|lt=Hiragana}}, it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient {{ill|Korean|en|Korean|WD=Q351637|lt=Korean}} administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as {{ill|sky blue|en|sky blue|WD=Q373160|lt=sky blue}} for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and {{ill|Red|en|Red|WD=Q3142|lt=Red}} for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}} and beyond, though none survive in their original form. 此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日

Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}, subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man’yōgana}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

'''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with {{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|WD=Q48332|lt=Hiragana}}, it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient {{ill|Korean|en|Korean|WD=Q351637|lt=Korean}} administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as {{ill|sky blue|en|sky blue|WD=Q373160|lt=sky blue}} for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and {{ill|Red|en|Red|WD=Q3142|lt=Red}} for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}} and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}, subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man’yōgana}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6139345]] Wikidata name: senmyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}, in contrast to {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=edictal texts}} written in {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese}}. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with {{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|WD=Q48332|lt=Hiragana}}, it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient {{ill|Korean|en|Korean|WD=Q351637|lt=Korean}} administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as {{ill|sky blue|en|sky blue|WD=Q373160|lt=sky blue}} for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and {{ill|Red|en|Red|WD=Q3142|lt=Red}} for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}} and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}, subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man’yōgana}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man’yōgana}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Sakumei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] [[az:Senmyo]][[ja:宣命]][[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]][[uk:Семмьо]][[zh:宣命 (日本)]] '''Senmyō''' (宣命) refers to imperial decrees issued in Japanese language using only {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}, in contrast to {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|WD=Q1284645|lt=edictal texts|1=Edict}} written in {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}}. This style of writing is called the '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Style]]''' (宣命体) or '''[[#Senmyō Style and Writing|Senmyō Writing]]''' (宣命書). The messenger who delivered these decrees was referred to as the '''Senmyō Messenger''' (宣命使), and the paper used for writing them was known as '''[[#Senmyō Paper|Senmyō Paper]]''' (宣命紙). The Senmyō style is considered the precursor to mixed kanji-kana writing (漢字仮名交じり文), making it a significant milestone in the development of Japanese script. == Overview == The term "Senmyō" literally means "imperial command," originally delivered orally and later recorded in Senmyō Style. In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, it was used for ceremonies such as [[imperial accession]], [[calendar reforms]], and [[imperial appointments]]. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, its use became limited to decrees related to [[imperial ministers]], [[posthumous Ranks]], [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], and [[imperial tombs]]. === Senmyō Style and Writing === The writing style used for Senmyō and [[Norito]]Senmyō style is still used in modern [[Norito]] and written by priests during festivals and prayers. is called Senmyō Style. The unique orthography, known as Senmyō Writing, uses large kanji for nouns and verb stems, while [[:en:particles|particles]], [[:en:auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verbs]], and inflectional endings are written smaller and positioned to the right using [[Man’yōgana]]. Specific kanji are consistently used for certain sounds: "を" as "乎," "の" as "乃," and "は" as "波." There are two types of Senmyō Style: 1. '''Large Script Style (大書体)''': All elements, including particles, are written in large characters. 2. '''Small Script Style (小書体)''': Particles and auxiliary elements are written in smaller characters. Senmyō Writing can be seen as "kanji-Man’yōgana mixed script." When Man’yōgana is replaced with {{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|WD=Q48332|lt=Hiragana|1=Hiragana}}, it closely resembles modern "kanji-kana mixed script," marking a pivotal development in Japanese script history.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.113-114. === Origins of Senmyō Style === It is hypothesized that ancient {{ill|Korean|en|Korean|WD=Q351637|lt=Korean|1=Korean}} administrative writing, known as [[idŭ]], influenced Senmyō Style. Korean is grammatically similar to Japanese, requiring the addition of particles and auxiliaries to classical Chinese texts. These additions, written at the end of Chinese sentence segments, resemble Senmyō Writing.Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' p.117. However, the advancement to writing small kana in Senmyō Small Script Style was a uniquely Japanese innovation.Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' p.187. === Senmyō Paper === {{See also|Hemp Paper}} The paper used for Senmyō documents was typically yellow hemp paper (黄麻紙), dyed with [[:en:amur cork tree|Amur cork]] to repel pests. For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as {{ill|sky blue|en|sky blue|WD=Q373160|lt=sky blue|1=Sky blue}} for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and {{ill|Red|en|Red|WD=Q3142|lt=Red|1=Red}} for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}} and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
瑞 乎 頂 尓 受賜 波理 | "The emperor’s (our) great command (Omi-koto) is hereby proclaimed.
Let the masses hear it.
On this 20th day of the 3rd month of the 9th year of the Tenpyō Shōhō era,
we receive and accept this great divine token
bestowed from the heavens." |} === Retired Emperor Go-Fushimi's Senmyō === After {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}} ascended to the throne via a Senmyō issued by {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}, subsequent emperors ascended under Senmyō decrees issued by retired emperors. The following example is a decree from Retired Emperor [[:en:Go-Fushimi|Go-Fushimi]] in 1331, appointing Prince Yutahito (later {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon|1=Emperor Kōgon}}). {{Quotation| '''Go-Fushimi’s Senmyō''' The Retired Emperor proclaims: To princes, royals, courtiers, officials, and the public, hear this decree: The former emperor has left his duties to the crown prince. This command ensures the stability of the realm. With a clear and righteous heart, aid the prince in governance, ensuring peace and stability for the empire. Let all hear and obey. }} == Notes == {{reflist}} == References == * Morioka Takashi, ''Illustrated Origins of Kana,'' Education Publishing, 2006, ISBN 978-4-316-80181-0. * Oshima Shoji, ''The Transmission of Kanji,'' Iwanami Shoten, 2006, ISBN 4-00-431031-8. * Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien,'' 4th edition, Iwanami Shoten, 1993. == See Also == * {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man’yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}} * {{ill|Japanese Calligraphy History|ja|日本の書道史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calligraphy History|1=history of Japanese calligraphy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42313217}} * {{ill|Sakumei|ja|策命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakumei|1=Q11603080|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603080}} {{translated page|ja|宣命|version=102721280|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宣命]] [[az:Senmyo]] [[ru:Сэммё (жанр)]] [[uk:Семмьо]] [[zh:宣命 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q6139345}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Diplomatics]] [[Category:Historical documents]] [[Category:Japanese calligraphy]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

For specific institutions, colored paper was used, such as {{ill|sky blue|en|sky blue|WD=Q373160|lt=sky blue|1=Sky blue}} for [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and {{ill|Red|en|Red|WD=Q3142|lt=Red|1=Red}} for [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo Shrines]].Shinmura Izuru, ''Kojien.'' == Examples of Senmyō == === Empress Kōken's Senmyō === The ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' includes 62 Senmyō from the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}} and beyond, though none survive in their original form. A transcription of a 757 CE decree from {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} is preserved in the [[Shōsōin]] documents.The decree is not included in the ''Shoku Nihongi.''Another transcription of Empress Kōken's Senmyō is found in the ''Shōsōin'' supplementary volumes. {| class="wikitable" |+ Partial Text of Empress Kōken's Senmyō |- ! Original !! Translation |- | 天皇 我 大命 良末等 宣 布 大命 乎
衆聞食 倍止 宣。
此 乃 天平勝宝九歳三月廿日
天 乃 賜 倍留 大 奈留
